skill.name: computer-vision-expert
skill.description: Expert guidance for computer vision architecture, object detection, segmentation, vision language models, calibration, and edge deployment planning.
skill.summary: Plan and review advanced computer vision systems for detection, segmentation, spatial analysis, and deployment.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings were evaluated against the Markdown content and dismissed as false positives. The SAM references are Segment Anything Model terminology, and the backticks on line 70 format related skill names rather than execute commands.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged line 70 as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The file is Markdown, and the backticks only format related skill names.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious text is inline Markdown content, not executable Ruby or shell code. No command invocation or script file exists in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: SAM Acronym
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged SAM references as Windows SAM database indicators. In context, SAM means Segment Anything Model for computer vision segmentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every cited SAM use appears in computer vision prose about segmentation, tracking, and VRAM usage. No Windows credential store access is described.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections occur in descriptive computer vision text. No hashing, encryption, credential handling, or cryptographic API usage appears.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains conceptual Markdown guidance only. I found no code path that performs cryptographic operations or processes secrets.

content.value_statement: Computer vision projects often fail when model choice, geometry, and deployment constraints are planned separately. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code reason through integrated vision architectures.
actual_capabilities: Explains detection pipeline design using YOLO26-oriented concepts.
Guides promptable segmentation workflows using SAM 3 terminology.
Connects vision language models with visual grounding and question answering tasks.
Reviews depth estimation, calibration, and reconstruction tradeoffs.
Plans deployment constraints for ONNX, TensorRT, NPU, and edge hardware.
limitations: Does not include executable code, datasets, or model weights.
Does not run inference or inspect images by itself.
References future or fast-moving model names that users should verify.
Requires project-specific hardware, latency, and accuracy requirements.

use_cases.title: Plan Inspection Pipelines
use_cases.description: Design a detection and segmentation flow for manufacturing inspection with clear latency and precision tradeoffs.

use_cases.title: Review Edge Deployment Options
use_cases.description: Compare export, quantization, and accelerator constraints before deploying a model on embedded hardware.

use_cases.title: Structure Visual Reasoning Features
use_cases.description: Map VLM, grounding, and VQA components into a product workflow that answers questions about visual scenes.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Vision Approach
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a computer vision approach for this task: [describe task]. Include model family, inputs, outputs, and evaluation metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Detection Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a real-time detection pipeline for [use case]. Include preprocessing, detector choice, postprocessing, failure modes, and deployment constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Combine Detection and Segmentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pipeline that combines object detection with promptable segmentation for [domain]. Explain data flow, prompt strategy, and validation checks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Vision System
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this computer vision architecture for accuracy, latency, calibration, privacy, and edge deployment risk: [architecture details].

output_examples.input: I need to inspect small defects on a moving production line.
output_examples.output: A recommended pipeline would combine high-shutter image capture, small-object detection, mask refinement, and per-class defect metrics.

output_examples.input: Can this work on an edge device with limited GPU memory?
output_examples.output: The response would compare model size, quantization options, input resolution, accelerator support, and acceptable latency targets.

output_examples.input: I want users to ask questions about camera images.
output_examples.output: The response would outline VLM selection, grounding requirements, answer validation, privacy controls, and fallback behavior.

best_practices: Define latency, accuracy, memory, and privacy targets before choosing a model.
Validate segmentation and detection outputs with domain-specific test images.
Plan camera calibration and lighting as part of the model architecture.
anti_patterns: Choosing a large model before measuring deployment constraints.
Using text prompts for ambiguous objects without visual grounding checks.
Treating calibration, lighting, and motion blur as model-only problems.

faq.question: Does this skill run computer vision models?
faq.answer: No. It provides planning and review guidance for computer vision systems.

faq.question: Can it help with YOLO-style detection systems?
faq.answer: Yes. It can reason about detection pipelines, latency, exports, and edge deployment tradeoffs.

faq.question: Can it help with segmentation prompts?
faq.answer: Yes. It can design text-guided and promptable segmentation workflows.

faq.question: Does it include training data?
faq.answer: No. Users must provide or collect their own datasets and labels.

faq.question: Is it useful for robotics or spatial systems?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers depth estimation, calibration, reconstruction, and visual SLAM concepts.

faq.question: Should model claims be verified?
faq.answer: Yes. Fast-moving model names, benchmarks, and hardware support should be checked before implementation.
